name: Aglaea
description: Aglaea, also known as Aglaia, is one of the three Charites (Graces) in Greek mythology, along with her sisters Euphrosyne and Thalia. She is the goddess of beauty, splendor, glory, magnificence, and adornment. As one of the Charites, Aglaea is responsible for bestowing charm and grace upon mortals and immortals alike. Her presence is associated with joy, festivity, and the blossoming of beauty. Some accounts identify her as the wife of Hephaestus, the god of the forge, following Aphrodite's departure. This union symbolizes the combination of beauty and craftsmanship. Aglaea is often depicted as a youthful and radiant figure, often dancing or attending to Aphrodite. She embodies the qualities of brilliance and adornment, contributing to the overall harmony and beauty of the world. She is a daughter of Zeus and either Eurynome or Eunomia. The Charites, including Aglaea, were attendants of Aphrodite and Hera, and were associated with the arts, music, and dance. They were believed to bring joy and goodwill wherever they went, and were highly revered in ancient Greece. Aglaea, in particular, represents the outward manifestation of beauty and the radiant splendor that enhances all things. Her role in the pantheon is to inspire admiration and to embody the ideals of grace and elegance.
subtitle: Goddess of Beauty, Splendor, and Adornment

keyMyths.title: The Charites' Birth
keyMyths.description: Aglaea and her sisters, Euphrosyne and Thalia, were born to Zeus and Eurynome (or Eunomia). Their birth brought joy and grace to the world, establishing their role as goddesses of beauty and charm.

keyMyths.title: Aglaea's Marriage
keyMyths.description: Aglaea is sometimes said to be married to Hephaestus, the god of the forge. This union represents the combination of beauty and craftsmanship, highlighting the importance of both in creating a harmonious world.

keyMyths.title: Attendants of Aphrodite
keyMyths.description: Aglaea and her sisters served as attendants to Aphrodite, the goddess of love and beauty. They assisted her in her duties and contributed to her radiant presence, embodying the qualities of grace and charm.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A genealogical account of the gods.
sources.text: A genealogical account of the gods.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: An epic poem about the Trojan War.
sources.text: An epic poem about the Trojan War.
